Entity_Description: Fraser Heights is a residential neighbourhood of roughly 25,000 people in north Surrey, British Columbia, within Metro Vancouver. It sits on a forested plateau above the south bank of the Fraser River, bounded by Highway 1 (the Trans-Canada Highway) to the south, near the Port Mann Bridge crossing to Coquitlam. About 26 km from downtown Vancouver, it developed largely from the 1980s onward as one of Surrey's more affluent enclaves, dominated by large single-family executive homes on treed lots.

Short_Description: Perched on a treed plateau above the south bank of the Fraser River, Fraser Heights is north Surrey's quiet executive enclave — cut off from the rest of the city by Highway 1 and anchored by the Port Mann Bridge at its western edge. Its large 1980s-to-2000s family homes sit under mature evergreens, which means shade, moisture, and roofs and exteriors that work hard through Metro Vancouver's long wet season. That is exactly the kind of {PROPERTY_TYPE} {COMPANY_NAME} knows well. As a local {BUSINESS_TYPE} with {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience, we deliver {SERVICE} matched to how Fraser Heights homes are actually built and where they actually sit. Long_Description:
Fraser Heights is easy to miss if you never leave Highway 1 — and that is exactly how many of its residents like it. The neighbourhood occupies a plateau in north Surrey, hemmed in by the Trans-Canada Highway to the south and the Fraser River bluff to the north, with the Port Mann Bridge carrying traffic across to Coquitlam at its western edge. That geography makes it one of Surrey's most self-contained communities: a pocket of winding residential streets, cul-de-sacs, and mature evergreens sitting above the river, home to roughly 25,000 people.

The housing stock here tells a consistent story. Unlike older parts of Surrey such as Bridgeview down by the river flats, Fraser Heights was built out largely from the 1980s through the 2000s as an executive-home neighbourhood — large single-family houses on generous treed lots, many with the complex rooflines and big exterior surface areas typical of that era. For {SERVICE}, that matters. Metro Vancouver's heavy November-to-March rainfall, combined with the deep tree cover on the plateau, means Fraser Heights properties deal with persistent shade, moss and algae growth, and drainage demands that a sunnier, more open subdivision simply does not face. A {BUSINESS_TYPE} that treats every Surrey home the same will miss what these properties actually need.

{COMPANY_NAME} works throughout Fraser Heights — from the streets overlooking the Fraser River to the homes backing the greenbelts near Highway 1 — and across the neighbouring communities that ring it: Bridgeview and central Surrey to the south and west, and Port Coquitlam, Coquitlam, and Pitt Meadows just across the river via the Port Mann and Golden Ears crossings. Because Fraser Heights sits at the foot of the Port Mann, crews can be on either side of the Fraser quickly, which keeps {RESPONSE_TIME} response realistic rather than a marketing line.

Households here are established, detail-oriented, and used to maintaining substantial properties; commuters cross the Port Mann daily to jobs throughout Canada's third-largest metropolitan economy. They expect work done properly the first time. That is the standard {COMPANY_NAME} holds itself to: {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}. With {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years serving Metro Vancouver and a {RATING} rating, we bring {SERVICE_CATEGORY} expertise shaped by the specific homes, trees, weather, and terrain of this plateau. Call {PHONE} or visit {WEBSITE} for a {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimate.
Local_Facts:
- Fraser Heights is a neighbourhood of about 25,000 residents in north Surrey, Metro Vancouver
- It sits on a plateau above the south bank of the Fraser River, roughly 26 km from downtown Vancouver
- Highway 1 (the Trans-Canada Highway) forms its southern boundary, separating it from the rest of Surrey
- The Port Mann Bridge at its western edge connects the neighbourhood across the Fraser River to Coquitlam
- Housing is predominantly large single-family executive homes built from the 1980s onward on treed lots
- Nearby communities include Bridgeview (6 km), Port Coquitlam (7 km), Pitt Meadows (8 km), and Coquitlam (9 km)
- The area has a mild oceanic climate with heavy November-March rainfall and warm, dry summers
